items.q: Does automation always destroy jobs overall?
items.correct: It displaces some manufacturing roles but also creates jobs in programming, maintenance, design and logistics; the overall effect varies by industry and over time.
items.misconception: Automation removes the need for any human workers in a factory.
items.random_wrong: Automation is banned in UK factories for safety reasons.
items.why: Students see robots replacing people one-for-one; balanced exam answers weigh job displacement against the new skilled roles automation creates.

items.q: What makes a product part of the Internet of Things?
items.correct: Embedded sensors and connectivity that let it send and receive data over the internet, like a thermostat you control remotely from a phone.
items.misconception: Any product containing electronics counts as the Internet of Things.
items.random_wrong: IoT products must include a built-in television screen.
items.why: Students equate 'electronic' with 'connected'; the defining feature is internet communication of data, not merely containing circuitry.

items.q: Is planned obsolescence the same as a product naturally wearing out?
items.correct: No — planned obsolescence is a deliberate design decision to limit useful life so customers buy replacements; natural wear is unavoidable degradation over time.
items.misconception: Planned obsolescence just means products eventually wear out by accident.
items.random_wrong: Planned obsolescence is the recycling stage of a product's life cycle.
items.why: The word 'planned' is the point students miss: it is an intentional commercial strategy with ethical and environmental consequences worth evaluating.

items.q: Which product is the best example of the Internet of Things?
items.correct: A thermostat controlled from a phone over the internet
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A thermostat controlled from a phone over the internet. “A battery-powered torch” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Designing a product to have a deliberately limited useful life
items.misconception: Recycling a product at end of life
items.random_wrong: Testing a product until it breaks
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Designing a product to have a deliberately limited useful life. “Recycling a product at end of life” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is a key benefit of automation in manufacturing?
items.misconception: It removes the need for product design
items.why: It's Consistent quality and continuous operation. “It removes the need for product design” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the aim of a circular economy?
items.correct: Keeping materials in use through reuse, repair and recycling
items.why: Correct answer: Keeping materials in use through reuse, repair and recycling. “Selling products in circular packaging” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
